results.reasoning: IR night clip shows a crested gecko perched on a branch/ledge making head and body movements with no food dish, prey, anchored repeated-licking posture, or skin removal visible, so it defaults to general moving.
IR footage of a gecko perched on a branch with slight head/body shifts and no visible dish-contact, prey, sustained fixed-spot licking, or skin-pulling evidence, so the default moving class applies.

Wide IR enclosure view shows the gecko locomoting/stretching across the terrarium over the frames with no tongue-to-paste contact, visible prey, anchored repeated licking, or shedding evidence, so it is moving.

Warm-lit clip shows the gecko resting draped along a cork branch away from any dish, with no tongue-to-food contact, prey, anchored repeated-licking posture, or detaching skin, so it defaults to moving.

Daylight clip shows the gecko turning and repositioning on the right end of a cork branch with normal coloration and no dish-contact, prey, sustained fixed-spot licking, or skin removal, so it is moving.

The gecko rests with its head hanging over the right edge of the branch but shows no demonstrated repeated licking of a fixed surface, no dish contact, no prey, and no skin removal, so ambiguity resolves to moving.

IR clip shows the gecko perched on an upper ledge behind a beam with minor head shifts and no visible dish-contact, prey, anchored repeated-licking posture, or shedding evidence, so it defaults to moving.

IR clip is dominated by enclosure decor with the camera panning, and only a pale curved tail-like gecko fragment is faintly identifiable with apparent scene motion, so per the tie-break preference it is moving rather than unseen.

IR night clip where a crested gecko enters and walks across the enclosure floor with continuous head and body locomotion, no food dish/prey contact, no anchored repeated licking, and pale markings are normal IR coloration, so it is general moving.

The gecko keeps its torso and limbs anchored on the rock perch while raising its head to repeatedly lick condensation droplets running down the glass wall, matching the body-anchored repeated-licking drinking posture with water visible.

A live insect is clearly visible on the surface and the gecko is oriented toward it in a fixed-gaze stalking posture then lunges/strikes at it, which is eating_prey per the prey-visible-plus-locked-attention rule (no hand or tool presenting it).

In this IR clip the gecko holds its head lowered directly at a clearly visible food dish with the snout at the food surface, sustained and repeated over roughly 25 seconds rather than a single inspection flick, indicating feeding on paste.

The gecko grips and climbs the black plastic rim/vent of a plastic tub, shifting position and looking around with no food dish, prey, anchored surface-licking, or peeling skin visible, so it is general moving.

A person holds a spoon loaded with a visible glob of paste and presents it to the gecko, which repeatedly licks the food from the spoon, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule for a feeding tool delivering food.
A hand presents a small spoon carrying a visible blob of CGD paste to the gecko's snout and the gecko licks food from it, which is human-assisted feeding via a tool and therefore hand_feeding.

Live crickets are clearly visible on the white surface and the gecko is oriented toward them in a stalking approach with locked attention, matching eating_prey with visible loose prey and no hand or tool presenting it.

In IR night footage the crested gecko emerges from behind a dark hide and climbs up and across the enclosure decor with full patterned body and long tail visible, showing general locomotion with no dish-feeding contact, anchored repeated licking, or peeling skin.

A large live cricket is clearly visible gripped in the gecko's mouth and being bitten/chewed throughout the clip, meeting the eating_prey requirement of visible prey plus active consumption.
In IR footage the gecko walks across the substrate and climbs up a slanted cork-bark branch, ascending out of frame with only its tail remaining, which is pure locomotion.

In IR footage the pale-morph gecko climbs on the glass and walks across the enclosure substrate with body oriented and legs striding, showing general movement and no sustained feeding or anchored licking.

Daytime footage shows a live prey insect clearly visible directly in front of the gecko, which is locked onto it in a stalking posture then strikes and captures it into its mouth.

A crested gecko in a white plastic tub curls and shifts its body/tail and turns its head across the frames with no food dish, prey, hand, or distinctly detached shed-skin flap visible, so the dominant behavior is general repositioning/locomotion.

A crested gecko clinging to a clear plastic perch orients toward and seizes a clearly visible dark insect (cricket/roach) that ends up gripped at its mouth, with no human hand or feeding tool present, matching prey capture/eating.

A cream-and-brown crested gecko walks and repositions across an open wood-laminate floor in daylight with no food dish, visible prey, hand, or distinctly peeling shed-skin flap, so the dominant behavior is locomotion.

A sharp daylight close-up shows a crested gecko on tile with a small dark insect (legs/antennae visible) held and chewed in its open mouth across the frames, with no hand or tool present, clearly indicating prey eating.

The gecko keeps its body anchored in one spot (stable relative to the cork bark and embossed floor text) and repeatedly extends its tongue to lick one fixed wet spot on the mist-droplet-covered enclosure floor (no food dish present), matching the anchored repeated-licking drinking posture, though the licked substance is ambiguous.

The gecko stands on top of a coconut hide with its head reaching down over the front edge, then turns around and climbs down toward the substrate, showing general locomotion with no sustained anchored tongue-to-surface licking or visible feeding contact.

Across the entire IR clip no gecko is identifiable anywhere inside the enclosure; the dominant bright central shape is the owner's reflection on the front glass (a knit-hatted person holding a phone), which the rubric excludes, so the animal is absent.

Same enclosure and camera setup as another clip, the entire sequence shows only the static tank interior with the human's phone/beanie reflection on the glass and no gecko present on the shelf, floor, dish, or hide.

The gecko approaches a small dish of dark CGD paste and its bright pink tongue is clearly seen contacting the paste at the dish edge with the head then lowered into the dish across successive frames, meeting the visible-dish plus tongue-to-paste contact requirement.

The gecko clings anchored to a condensation-covered glass wall and repeatedly extends its pink tongue across multiple frames to lick water droplets off the glass, the textbook body-anchored repeated-licking drinking pattern.

A human hand holds tweezers with a prey insect on the tip and presents it directly to the gecko's mouth, and the gecko bites and takes the food from the tool, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule which overrides eating_prey.

Color close-up shows distinct translucent pieces of old skin visibly lifting, peeling, and hanging partially detached from the gecko's flanks, legs, and tail across all frames, meeting the shedding evidence requirement.

A large distinct piece of translucent old skin is draped and detached from the body while the gecko twists and pulls at it with its mouth, showing active skin removal.

A large distinct membranous piece of old skin with clear separating edges hangs partially detached over the gecko's neck and flank while it works to remove it, a clear active-shedding signal (not IR pale-color artifact since coloration is visible).

A finger-held red spoon loaded with paste is presented to the gecko's snout in the majority of frames and the gecko orients toward and mouths the spoon tip, so the dominant activity is human-assisted feeding per the OOD rule.

The gecko on a branch has multiple distinct white translucent flaps of old skin peeling and hanging detached from its flank, legs and toes and is actively pulling them off with its mouth, clear ecdysis.

Live crickets are clearly visible on the substrate in the frame and the gecko shows focused prey-locked stalking posture, head down and body oriented toward a visible cricket, matching eating_prey (stalking with visible prey).

Under a hide in daylight the gecko holds and chews a distinct white translucent piece of detached old skin at its mouth with more shed pieces on the substrate, which is shedding (chewing shed skin), not eating_paste since no food dish or prey is present.

The crested gecko is inside a tub swarming with live crickets, moving among them in a lowered hunting posture with focused attention on the visible prey, and in one frame has a cricket at its mouth in a capture/bite.

The gecko is out on an open white table with no food, water, dish, or prey present, repositioning across frames and only doing sporadic isolated tongue flicks (chemoreception/grooming), which is general movement, not drinking or eating.

In this IR night clip two crested geckos continuously walk and reposition around the enclosure floor, dish, and cork branch without any sustained body-anchored repeated licking of one spot, no tongue-to-dish feeding, and no detached shed skin (pale spotting is normal IR-washed coloration).

In IR night footage the crested gecko descends from the cork ledge and walks/climbs across the enclosure floor and up the right side, with the bright white patches being normal light-morph coloration in IR rather than any detached shedding skin, and no anchored repeated licking.

A live cricket (its legs and antennae clearly visible) is right at the gecko's mouth on the paper-towel substrate with the head oriented toward and engaging the prey, and no hand or tool presents it, matching prey-visible focused feeding.

A human hand holds a syringe loaded with paste whose tip is presented directly to the gecko's mouth and the gecko licks/takes the food from the tool, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule over eating_paste.

The gecko stays anchored against a condensation-covered clear wall and repeatedly extends its tongue to lick water droplets off one fixed spot on the glass, matching the body-anchored repeated-licking drinking posture.

A person's finger merely touches and handles a small gecko on a paper towel with no food, prey, or feeding tool offered, so the hand_feeding rule does not apply and the gecko is just being handled/shifting.

The gecko is positioned on driftwood in a planted terrarium with head movement and an open-mouth gape, but no identifiable live prey is visible in the frame, so eating_prey cannot be confirmed and it defaults to moving.

In IR footage the gecko climbs on the front glass (large out-of-focus pale form) and moves up into the top corners of the enclosure, with pale IR appearance being morph coloration and no anchored licking or detached skin.

A visible dish of brown CGD paste is beside the gecko's head and its pink tongue is repeatedly extended to contact the opaque paste surface across frames, satisfying visible-dish plus repeated tongue-to-paste contact.
In this IR night clip the gecko walks across the substrate from behind a branch, passing near a small dish, with no anchored repeated-licking or tongue-to-food contact, so it is general locomotion.

In this IR clip the gecko travels from the front glass to the back corner and settles with its head near (not licking) a small dish, showing locomotion with no tongue-to-paste contact or sustained anchored licking.
In this bright close-up the gecko repeatedly extends its tongue into a clearly visible yellow dish of opaque brown paste, feeding autonomously with no hand or tool present.
A human-held purple feeding spoon loaded with brown paste is presented directly to the gecko's mouth and the gecko takes food from it, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.
In this IR clip a pale (normal-morph) gecko traverses the floor and climbs the decor beside a water dispenser without an anchored, sustained repeated-licking drinking posture, so it is moving.
A distinct translucent piece of old skin is peeling and hanging detached over the gecko's face while its open mouth actively pulls and chews the shed skin, which is active ecdysis.
Live superworm prey is clearly visible in the container and the gecko orients its head onto a worm with a focused strike/grab posture, matching prey-locked feeding.

A high-white morph gecko (whose pale markings are normal coloration, not shed skin) perches with its head up near the container edge making small head movements, with no feeding, drinking, or detached-skin evidence.
Daytime color footage shows the crested gecko with distinct translucent old-skin flaps visibly peeling and hanging partially detached from its head and body, the diagnostic sign of active ecdysis.

Grayscale IR night footage shows the gecko descending the cork bark and walking across the substrate with no food contact, anchored licking, or detached skin flap, so pale IR coloration aside this is general locomotion.
A pink feeding spoon loaded with brown paste is held up to the gecko and presented to its mouth throughout the clip, a human-assisted food-delivery action that triggers the hand_feeding OOD rule.

The gecko stays anchored on a ledge behind heavily condensation-covered glass and extends its red tongue to lick the wet glass surface, the body-anchored surface-licking posture that defines drinking.

Feeding tweezers appear only in the opening 2 of 7 frames while the dominant portion shows the gecko autonomously chewing a clearly visible cricket, so by the dominant-activity rule this reads as eating_prey (hand_feeding is a plausible alternative given the initial tweezers delivery).

Grayscale IR night footage shows the gecko traversing from the cork bark ledge to the right-side glass and climbing, with IR overexposure rather than any detached skin flap and no sustained anchored licking, so the dominant behavior is locomotion.
Daytime color footage shows a large distinct sheet of translucent old skin peeling and hanging off the gecko's back, shoulders, and limbs with clear separating edges, an unambiguous active shed.

Grayscale IR night footage shows the gecko partially entering from the lower-left with an identifiable tail/limb moving across the frame near the dish and then exiting, which per the brief-visibility rule counts as moving rather than unseen.
A human hand presents a wooden feeding stick loaded with paste directly to the gecko's mouth and the gecko orients toward and licks food from it, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.

In this IR night clip a pale gecko is on the cork-bark shelf shifting position (the central bright blob is decor with IR glare, not shed skin), showing general locomotion with no dish contact, anchored licking, or detached skin flap.

The gecko has its snout buried in a clearly visible dish of tan CGD paste with the mouth open in direct contact and paste smeared on its muzzle across multiple frames, indicating sustained feeding on the paste.
The gecko walks across a dark blanket in a clear striding posture while a bare human hand merely rests nearby with no food being offered, so per the OOD rule it is judged as locomotion.
In this IR clip the gecko walks across the enclosure floor and climbs up the cork-bark branch, changing position substantially with no food-dish contact, anchored licking, or peeling skin.

Distinct translucent pieces of old skin are visibly peeling and hanging detached around the gecko's head and limbs in daylight color footage while it pulls/chews at the skin, which is active ecdysis rather than eating.

In this IR clip a pied-marked gecko walks across the substrate floor and along the front glass then climbs the branch, with the white markings being normal morph coloration and no shed skin, dish contact, or anchored licking.

This motion-triggered IR clip shows the gecko present and shifting on the cork-bark shelf (the bright central object is a glare-lit decor ornament, not shed skin), so with the body identifiable and no specialized behavior it defaults to moving over unseen.

In grayscale IR night footage a pale-appearing crested gecko enters the frame and walks across the substrate toward the corner past two untouched dishes, showing general locomotion with no anchored repeated licking, no tongue-to-food contact, and no detached shed skin.

IR night footage shows the crested gecko climbing down a cork branch and moving across the substrate right up to the lens, continuous locomotion with no fixed-spot repeated licking, no food-dish contact, and no peeling skin flap.

A human hand holds a syringe loaded with yellow paste and presents it directly to the crested gecko, whose tongue makes contact with the paste at the tip, meeting the OOD hand_feeding rule (a feeding tool delivering food to the gecko).

IR night footage shows the crested gecko entering and moving through the enclosure (tail then body sprawling into the foreground) with no anchored repeated licking, no contact with the untouched dishes, and pale patches that are normal IR coloration rather than shed skin.

After setup shots of a prey plate, tweezers/forceps hold an insect and present it to the crested gecko, which lunges, bites, and chews it from the tool, matching the OOD hand_feeding rule (feeding tool delivering prey to the gecko).

A bare human hand pets and strokes a high-white crested gecko's back and head with no food present, so per the handling exception it is not hand_feeding, and the gecko is merely being handled and shifting position toward a branch (moving).

A patterned crested gecko rests on a paper towel in a deli tub with only minor head turning and a brief tongue flick, showing no visible food source, no sustained body-anchored repeated licking of one spot, and no distinct detached shed-skin flap.

An orange crested gecko held on a hand has a distinct whitish flap of old skin visibly peeling and hanging detached from its flank and foreleg across several frames, the diagnostic evidence of active ecdysis (shedding).

In IR night footage the crested gecko emerges from a dark hide and crosses the front of the enclosure, its head only briefly passing near the white dispenser without any observed sustained tongue-to-paste contact or anchored repeated licking, so the dominant behavior is locomotion.
In clear daytime footage a distinct translucent piece of old skin is visibly peeling and hanging partially detached from the gecko's head, neck, and leg during active ecdysis.

In IR night footage the high-white gecko walks across the substrate toward the food dish and dispenser in continuous locomotion, with no observable tongue-to-food contact or body-anchored repeated licking.
In daytime footage a small crested gecko crawls across a white paper surface, continuously changing position and pose with no food, prey, water-licking, or shedding skin present.

In IR night footage the gecko approaches the dispenser and raises its head toward it, but the body keeps moving (climbing in then leaving) without a clear sustained anchored repeated-licking posture or visible tongue-to-paste contact, so it defaults to moving.
In IR night footage the gecko climbs on the front glass and then descends and walks across the substrate in continuous locomotion, with no sustained anchored licking of a single spot.
In bright daytime footage the crested gecko is locked onto a clearly visible live cricket with a low stalking posture and lunges to strike it, with no human hand or tool involved.

In IR night footage the high-white gecko walks across the substrate and then climbs up onto the cork branch, showing continuous locomotion with no anchored repeated licking or visible tongue-to-food contact.
A large distinct sheet of translucent old skin is peeling off and hanging detached from the body while the gecko actively pulls and rubs it loose with head/mouth movements across the clip.
A human hand holds a small feeding spoon loaded with brown CGD paste and presents it directly to the gecko, whose snout contacts the paste on the tool, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.

In IR night footage the gecko walks across the enclosure floor and then settles curled near the cork bark with only minor position shifts, showing no tongue-to-food contact, anchored repeated licking, or distinct peeling skin.
The gecko stays anchored under a hide with its head over a yellow dish of brown paste and repeatedly extends its tongue to make visible contact with the paste surface across multiple frames.

IR footage is dominated by camera glare with the gecko pressed near the glass; its textured body is visible with slight repositioning but no anchored repeated licking, food contact, or distinct peeling skin, so it defaults to moving.

In IR night footage the gecko climbs onto and over the clear water dispenser and moves around the substrate, showing continuous locomotion rather than anchored repeated licking or any food-contact behavior.

In IR night footage the gecko crawls along the substrate under the cork bark and climbs up onto the bark ledge, clear locomotion with no anchored repeated licking, food-dish contact, or distinct peeling skin.
A close-up shows the gecko anchored at a yellow dish of brown CGD paste with its tongue clearly extended and repeatedly lapping the visible paste surface across several frames.

In color footage a distinct pale translucent sheet of old skin is peeling and hanging off the gecko's back and neck with a clear separating edge while the mouth pulls and chews at it (frames 004-010), textbook active ecdysis.

IR night clip where the gecko intermittently noses the cork branch a few times but lowers its head between touches and then climbs up the branch and moves actively for the majority of the clip, so the dominant behavior is locomotion rather than a sustained anchored lick.

IR night clip in which the gecko descends from the branch, crosses the substrate, tucks behind the branch and re-emerges resting its head on the substrate, showing repositioning locomotion with no dish contact or sustained anchored repeated-licking posture.

The gecko's pink tongue is clearly touching an opaque orange fruit-paste blob on the enclosure rim with repeated licks evidenced by the growing smear, and no hand or feeding tool is present, so it is autonomous paste feeding.

A human hand holds a syringe with food paste at the tip and presents it to the gecko, which repeatedly raises its head and licks the paste directly from the syringe tip, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.

A food dish holding visible tan CGD paste is present and the gecko keeps its snout lowered into the dish at the paste surface with front feet on the rim for the entire clip, indicating sustained active feeding at the dish.

Viewed through a heavily condensation-fogged tub wall the gecko is identifiable as a gecko and its position shifts slightly across frames, but no dish, prey, shed flap, or anchored licking behavior is discernible, so it defaults to moving.

A pink feeding spoon held by an off-frame handle presents brown CGD paste to the gecko, which actively licks the paste off the spoon with its tongue visible on the paste, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.

In IR night footage the crested gecko travels continuously across the enclosure, climbing over the dark hide toward the water dispenser with its whole body displacing every frame and no anchored repeated licking or detached skin flap (pale patches are the light morph under IR).

IR night clip shows the light-morph gecko climbing over the hide from the left, crossing the top of the water dispenser, then descending the right side head-down toward the substrate, i.e. sustained locomotion with no anchored licking and no distinct peeling skin.

After a mostly empty IR scene, a gecko enters and walks across the substrate near a small dish but shows no observable tongue-to-food contact and no anchored repeated licking, so proximity to the dish alone makes this general locomotion.
A human hand holds a syringe tipped with tan CGD paste and presents it directly to the gecko's mouth, and the gecko takes/licks the paste from the tool, triggering the hand_feeding OOD rule.

In daylight color footage the gecko has distinct detached/peeling pieces of whitish old skin draped over its head and back while it opens its mouth and uses a raised foot to pull and chew the shed, which is active ecdysis.

The gecko stays anchored on a branch over a ceramic dish holding opaque brown paste and repeatedly holds its snout down into the paste surface across the whole clip, matching sustained eating from a visible food dish rather than a brief inspect-and-leave.

Body-anchored on a vine, the gecko holds still and repeatedly extends its pink tongue to lick condensation droplets off the misted glass wall, an external non-dish surface, matching the drinking posture.

The gecko is merely being handled on a person's hand with no food or feeding tool offered, and it climbs off onto the plant stems, so per the OOD carve-out this is locomotion (moving), not hand_feeding.

A syringe (feeding tool) tipped with fruit paste is held directly to the gecko's mouth and the gecko licks and takes the paste from it (mouth open on the syringe in the middle frames), which the OOD rule classifies as hand_feeding.

In grayscale IR night footage the crested gecko climbs and walks across the cork-bark ramp through the later frames with no dish contact, no detached shed skin, and only normal pale IR coloration, so this is general locomotion.

In color footage a large translucent piece of old skin is visibly peeling, lifting, and hanging detached from the neck and torso while the gecko twists to work it off, which is unambiguous active ecdysis.
In grayscale IR night footage the gecko moves across the enclosure substrate/floor with no body-anchored repeated licking of a dish and no detached shed skin, so the dominant behavior is locomotion.
The gecko keeps its body anchored in one spot over a blue water dish and repeatedly dips its head to lick the water surface, with the tongue visibly contacting the water, which is drinking.

A human hand is merely handling and holding the gecko with no food, prey, paste, or feeding tool offered, and the gecko walks and repositions across the palm and wrist, so by the OOD rule this is moving rather than hand_feeding.

A syringe (feeding tool) with a glob of paste on its tip is repeatedly presented to the gecko's snout and the gecko licks/takes the paste across most of the clip, which the OOD rule classifies as hand_feeding overriding eating_paste.
In color footage a distinct piece of loose translucent old skin is detached around the gecko's front foot and body and the gecko turns to pull and chew the shed skin off, confirming active ecdysis.

The gecko is anchored head-down at a wall-mounted dish/bowl across all frames, but no tongue-to-paste contact and no clear repeated-licking pattern is observable and the bowl content is indeterminate, so per the rules head-over-dish posture alone defaults to moving.

Multiple live crickets are clearly visible on the substrate and the gecko is locked onto and striking/capturing a cricket right at its mouth, which is autonomous prey hunting with no hand or tool present.

In grayscale IR night footage the gecko continuously climbs over the dark hide and walks across the enclosure, and its pale appearance is IR color washout with no distinct detached or peeling skin flap, so it is moving not shedding.

The gecko holds its body anchored in one spot and repeatedly extends its tongue to lick a dark decor surface heavily covered in visible misting/water droplets, matching the body-anchored sustained repeated-licking drinking posture.

In grayscale IR footage the gecko climbs over the hide and onto/over the water dispenser and then out of frame in continuous locomotion, never settling into an anchored repeated-licking posture, and its pale IR tone is not shed skin.

The gecko clings anchored to a condensation-covered glass wall and repeatedly extends its tongue to lick the wet glass across successive frames, which is the body-anchored repeated-licking drinking pattern on a non-paste surface.

An opaque tan CGD paste is clearly visible inside the orange food dish and the gecko's head is anchored at the paste with its tongue making repeated contact with the paste surface across multiple frames.

A dish of opaque brown CGD paste is clearly visible and the gecko repeatedly extends its tongue down into the paste with a visible paste strand, showing sustained tongue-to-paste contact throughout the clip.

In the final grayscale IR frames a crested gecko appears from the foreground and actively climbs up and over the water dispenser bottle, showing clear locomotion with no anchored repeated-licking, no visible food/prey, no hand/tool, and no distinct detached/peeling shed-skin flap (its pale patterning is normal IR-washed morph coloration), so the dominant behavior is moving.
